title: The battery that steadied a grid (2017)
what: South Australia installed the Hornsdale Power Reserve, then the world's largest lithium-ion battery, beside a wind farm.
why: It responds in a fraction of a second to grid disturbances, a job that once needed spinning fossil plants on standby.
care: It proved grid-scale batteries can pay for themselves and sparked a global wave of similar projects.

title: Fusion 'ignition' in a lab (2022)
what: In December 2022 the U.S. National Ignition Facility produced a fusion reaction that released more energy than the lasers delivered to the fuel capsule.
why: It was the first lab fusion to pass that 'break-even' line, a milestone scientists chased for decades.
care: It's a proof of physics, not a power plant — but it's the strongest sign yet that fusion energy is possible.

title: America's first new reactors in a generation
what: Plant Vogtle in Georgia brought two new reactors online in 2023 and 2024 — the first newly built U.S. reactors in about three decades.
why: They came in years late and far over budget, showing how hard large nuclear builds have become in the West.
care: Whether nuclear can be built on time and on budget is central to many countries' clean-energy plans.

title: Small modular reactors
what: SMRs are compact reactors built in factories and shipped to site, rather than constructed piece by piece like giant plants.
why: Standardised, smaller units aim to cut the cost overruns and long build times that plague big reactors.
care: If they deliver, they could put reliable carbon-free power near towns, data centres, and remote industry.

title: The first SMR design clears U.S. regulators
what: NuScale's small modular reactor became the first SMR design certified by the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission.
why: Regulatory approval is a huge hurdle; certifying the design opened the door for utilities to consider building it.
care: It's a key step toward a nuclear industry that builds reactors like products, not one-off megaprojects.

title: Geothermal goes where there's no volcano
what: Enhanced geothermal systems use drilling techniques borrowed from oil and gas to create heat reservoirs in hot, dry rock far from natural hot springs.
why: Traditional geothermal needs rare natural conditions; this could unlock clean, always-on heat almost anywhere.
care: It offers steady renewable power that, unlike solar and wind, runs day and night regardless of weather.

title: Splitting water with clean power
what: Green hydrogen is made by using renewable electricity to split water into hydrogen and oxygen in an electrolyser.
why: The hydrogen can store energy, fuel heavy transport, or replace fossil gas in industries hard to electrify, like steel.
care: It's a candidate for cleaning up the parts of the economy that batteries and wires can't easily reach.

title: Virtual power plants
what: Software links thousands of home batteries, solar panels, and smart appliances so they can act together like a single power station.
why: Coordinated, they can feed power back or trim demand at peak times, easing strain without building new plants.
care: Your future home battery or water heater could quietly earn you money by helping balance the grid.

title: Cars that power the grid back
what: Vehicle-to-grid technology lets an electric car's battery send energy back to a home or the grid when it's parked and plugged in.
why: A nation's parked EVs hold an enormous amount of storage that mostly sits idle.
care: Your car could one day help power your house during an outage or earn credit by supporting the grid at peak times.

title: Batteries that last for days
what: New chemistries like iron-air aim to store energy for many hours or even days, far longer than today's lithium batteries.
why: Cheap multi-day storage could cover long stretches of cloudy, windless weather that short batteries can't.
care: It may be the missing piece that lets wind and solar reliably run a grid around the clock.

title: Batteries made from salt
what: Sodium-ion batteries replace scarce lithium with abundant, cheap sodium, trading some energy density for lower cost.
why: Sodium is everywhere and far cheaper, easing worries about lithium supply and price.
care: They could make home and grid storage cheaper, even if they stay too heavy for long-range cars.

title: The solid-state battery race
what: Solid-state batteries swap the flammable liquid inside a normal battery for a solid material that carries the charge.
why: They promise more range, faster charging, and less fire risk — but are hard to manufacture at scale.
care: If perfected, they could be the leap that makes electric cars charge as fast as filling a tank.

title: The next generation of solar cells
what: Perovskite materials, often layered on top of silicon, keep setting new records for how much sunlight a cell can convert.
why: Stacking two materials captures more of the spectrum, pushing efficiency past what silicon alone can reach.
care: Higher-efficiency panels mean more power from the same roof — and cheaper solar for everyone.

title: Fusion leaves the government lab
what: Private companies are building compact fusion machines, some using powerful new high-temperature superconducting magnets.
why: They aim to reach fusion power faster and cheaper than giant public projects by going small and iterating quickly.
care: Billions in private investment have turned fusion from a distant dream into a genuine commercial race.

title: China's ultra-high-voltage lines
what: China has built ultra-high-voltage links operating above a million volts to carry power thousands of kilometres across the country.
why: They move electricity from remote dams, solar, and wind in the west to the crowded, power-hungry east.
care: They show how a continent-sized clean grid might move renewable power from where it's made to where it's needed.

title: Offshore wind on a giant scale
what: Projects like the UK's Dogger Bank are building the world's largest offshore wind farms far out at sea.
why: Steady ocean winds and ever-larger turbines let a single project power millions of homes.
care: Offshore wind is becoming a backbone of clean power for coastal nations on several continents.

title: Solar parks the size of cities
what: Enormous solar parks such as Bhadla in India's Thar Desert cover tens of square kilometres with millions of panels.
why: Building solar in vast, sun-soaked clusters drives the cost of clean electricity to record lows.
care: Solar is now the cheapest electricity in history in many sunny regions, thanks to projects at this scale.

title: Capturing carbon from power plants
what: Carbon capture equipment strips carbon dioxide from a power plant's exhaust before it reaches the air, then stores it underground.
why: It could cut emissions from existing fossil plants, though it's costly and uses some of the plant's own power.
care: It's a contested but real tool for cleaning up power and heavy industry that can't easily switch fuels.

title: Electronics that steady a green grid
what: Grid-forming inverters let batteries and solar set the grid's voltage and frequency themselves, mimicking the steadying role of spinning generators.
why: As fossil plants retire, the grid loses their natural stability; smart electronics can recreate it.
care: This quiet upgrade is key to running a reliable grid on mostly wind, solar, and batteries.

title: Keeping old reactors running
what: Many countries are extending the licences of existing reactors, and some are even restarting recently closed plants.
why: Existing reactors are a large, ready source of carbon-free power that's cheaper to keep than to replace.
care: Saving today's reactors may matter as much for the climate as building new ones.

title: Getting paid to use less at peak
what: Demand-response programmes pay homes and businesses to cut or shift electricity use when the grid is strained.
why: Trimming demand for a few hours can be cheaper and cleaner than firing up an extra power plant.
care: Signing up can lower your bill while helping prevent blackouts on the hottest, tightest days.

title: Microgrids that stand alone
what: A microgrid is a small, local network — perhaps a campus or town — that can disconnect and run on its own generation and storage.
why: When the main grid fails, a microgrid can keep critical services like hospitals powered.
care: They're a resilience tool as extreme weather makes long, widespread outages more common.

title: Heat pumps move heat instead of making it
what: A heat pump uses electricity to move heat from outside into your home, delivering several units of heat per unit of electricity.
why: Because it moves heat rather than burning fuel, it can be far more efficient than a furnace or electric heater.
care: Switching heating to efficient electric heat pumps is one of the biggest ways homes can cut energy use and emissions.

title: Farming under solar panels
what: Agrivoltaics places solar panels above crops or grazing land so the same ground produces both food and electricity.
why: Panels can shade and shelter some crops while the land keeps earning from farming.
care: It eases the worry that solar farms must compete with agriculture for scarce land.

title: Mining old batteries instead of the earth
what: New recycling plants recover lithium, nickel, and cobalt from spent batteries to make new ones.
why: Recycling eases demand for fresh mining and keeps hazardous materials out of landfills.
care: As today's electric cars age, recycling will decide how clean and self-sufficient the battery supply chain becomes.

title: Wind turbines that float
what: Floating platforms let wind turbines be anchored in deep water, far from shore where fixed foundations can't reach.
why: Most of the world's strongest offshore winds are over water too deep for traditional turbines.
care: It opens vast new ocean areas — off Japan, California, and Europe — to clean wind power.

title: Data centres reshape power demand
what: The boom in cloud computing and artificial intelligence is driving a surge in electricity demand from data centres.
why: After years of flat demand, this growth is pushing utilities to build new generation and even revive nuclear plants.
care: The digital services you use every day are now a real force shaping how much power the world needs.

title: Charging an EV in minutes
what: High-power chargers can now add a couple of hundred kilometres of range to many electric cars in roughly 15–20 minutes.
why: Fast, widespread charging tackles the 'range anxiety' that slowed electric-car adoption.
care: As charging approaches the speed of a fuel stop, the last big practical hurdle to EVs keeps shrinking.

title: Grid batteries go mainstream
what: Regions like California and Texas have added thousands of megawatts of grid batteries that now routinely cover the evening demand peak.
why: Batteries soak up cheap midday solar and release it at sunset, replacing gas 'peaker' plants.
care: It's a live demonstration that storage, not just generation, is becoming a core part of the grid.

title: Repowering ageing wind farms
what: Older wind farms are being 'repowered' — swapping early turbines for fewer, far larger and more efficient modern ones.
why: A handful of today's turbines can out-produce dozens of 1990s machines on the same windy site.
care: It squeezes much more clean power from land already approved for wind, without hunting for new sites.
